claim.content:
At the fermion-condensation quantum phase transition point, where the zero-temperature effective mass diverges and the Landau effective-mass equation becomes homogeneous, the low-temperature solution for a homogeneous heavy-fermion liquid satisfies
      $
      M^{*}(T)\propto T^{-2/3}.
      $
      Therefore, in the non-Fermi-liquid regime near the fermion-condensation quantum phase transition, the quasiparticle effective mass decreases with increasing temperature according to the power law $T^{-2/3}$.

conclusion.content:
At the fermion-condensation quantum phase transition point, where the zero-temperature effective mass diverges and the Landau effective-mass equation becomes homogeneous, the low-temperature solution for a homogeneous heavy-fermion liquid satisfies
      $
      M^{*}(T)\propto T^{-2/3}.
      $
      Therefore, in the non-Fermi-liquid regime near the fermion-condensation quantum phase transition, the quasiparticle effective mass decreases with increasing temperature according to the power law $T^{-2/3}$.

premises.content: At the fermion-condensation quantum phase transition point, the first two momentum derivatives of the single-particle spectrum vanish at the Fermi momentum $p_F$. The spectrum therefore has an inflection point at $p_F$, and the lowest nonvanishing term in the Taylor expansion of $\varepsilon(\mathbf{p})$ about $p_F$ is proportional to $(p-p_F)^3$.

Writing the thermal deviation of the quasiparticle distribution as $n_1(\mathbf{p})=n(\mathbf{p},T)-n(\mathbf{p})$, where $n(\mathbf{p})$ is the zero-temperature step function, transforms the Landau effective-mass equation into
$\displaystyle \frac{1}{M^{*}(T)}=\frac{1}{M^{*}}+\int \frac{\mathbf{p}_{F}\mathbf{p}_{1}}{p_{F}^{3}}F(\mathbf{p}_{F},\mathbf{p}_{1})\frac{\partial n_{1}(p_{1},T)}{\partial p_{1}}\frac{d\mathbf{p}_{1}}{(2\pi)^{3}}.$
At the fermion-condensation quantum phase transition, the zero-temperature effective mass diverges, so the first term on the right-hand side vanishes and the equation becomes homogeneous. The low-temperature solution of this homogeneous equation is
$M^{*}(T)\propto T^{-2/3}$.
[13]

steps.reasoning:
Treat as known the exact inverse effective mass relation obtained earlier (conclusion 1), and introduce the quasiparticle deviation from the step distribution $n_1(\mathbf{p},T)=n(\mathbf{p},T)-n(\mathbf{p})$, where $n(\mathbf{p})$ denotes the zero-temperature Fermi-step distribution and $n(\mathbf{p},T)$ denotes the finite-temperature occupation; substitute this into the inverse effective mass relation to obtain the temperature-dependent form
$
\displaystyle \frac{1}{M^{*}(T)}=\frac{1}{M^{*}}+\int \frac{\mathbf{p}_F\cdot\mathbf{p}_1}{p_F^3}\,F(\mathbf{p}_F,\mathbf{p}_1)\,\frac{\partial n_1(p_1,T)}{\partial p_1}\,\frac{d\mathbf{p}_1}{(2\pi)^3},
$
where $M^{*}(T)$ denotes the effective mass at temperature $T$ and $M^{*}$ on the right-hand side is the zero-temperature effective mass in the absence of the $n_1$ correction.

Invoke the FCQPT condition where the zero-temperature effective mass $M^{*}$ diverges (i.e., $1/M^{*}\to0$); under this condition the first term on the right-hand side vanishes and the equation becomes homogeneous in $M^{*}$, implying that the temperature dependence of $M^{*}$ is determined entirely by the integral term involving $\partial n_1/\partial p_1$.
[10]

Solve (or note the asymptotic solution of) the homogeneous equation for $M^{*}(T)$ in the FCQPT limit and state the resulting asymptotic temperature dependence
$
\displaystyle M^{*}(T)\propto T^{-2/3},
$
thereby identifying the non-Fermi liquid (NFL) regime near FCQPT in which the quasiparticle effective mass decreases with temperature following the $T^{-2/3}$ power law.
[13]

Comment that when the system is located before FCQPT (i.e., $M^{*}$ finite at $T=0$), the second term on the right-hand side is small at low $T$ and the system shows Landau Fermi liquid (LFL) behavior $M^{*}(T)\simeq M^{*}+a_1 T^2$ with constant $a_1$, and that upon raising temperature the system passes through a transition regime and then re-enters the NFL $T^{-2/3}$ regime for $T\gtrsim T_M$; thus the $T^{-2/3}$ law characterizes the NFL regime at FCQPT.

motivating_questions.content: Measurements on HF compounds reveal pronounced NFL temperature dependences of thermodynamic coefficients whose origins were not captured by standard LFL theory. A specific quantitative asymptotic law for $M^{*}(T)$ at the critical point was absent: without such a law one cannot predict temperature power laws of observable quantities (for example, $C/T$, susceptibility, or resistivity prefactors) that directly depend on $M^{*}$. Deriving an explicit asymptotic exponent and its relation to the singular solution of the self-consistent equation for $M^{*}$ was therefore required to link microscopic Landau-function feedback to observable NFL power laws.
